entry.title: Declaration
entry.content:

Academic Honesty Statement

  • Signature: Xinyi Zhou

entry.title: The Aesthetic Paradox: Ugliness as a Tool in Dance and Performance
entry.content:
The Subjectivity and Objectivity of Beauty

From ancient and medieval times, most thinkers of the West viewed beauty as an attribute of things in themselves. During the modern era, the subjective outlook on beauty has become predominant.

"Beauty is no quality in things themselves: It exists merely in the mind which contemplates them; and each mind perceives a different beauty." -- David Hume's Quote (from "Of the Standard of Taste," 1742)

"The judgement of taste is therefore not a cognitive judgement, and so not logical, but is aesthetic—which means that it is one whose determining ground cannot be other than subjective." -- Immanuel Kant's Quote (from "Critique of Judgment," 1790)

Hume and Kant's discussion on the standard of taste is referred to as the "Antinomy of Taste." Speaking specifically about dance, aesthetics of art could be viewed as a subjective attitude, which every human being assumes toward the objective phenomena. The aesthetic sense is subtly influenced by upbringing, life experiences, educational background, politics, and economics. Although the standards that most people judge something to be beautiful or ugly are roughly similar, we can't categorically do so for an act of any performance.

Therefore, the following discussion about ugliness is based solely on either my personal aesthetic perspective or the general aesthetic standards commonly held by the public.

The Role of Ugliness in Dance or Performance Expression

Beauty and ugliness, good and evil, admiration and infatuation, vows and betrayal, power and possession, fate and resistance, original sin and redemption—all oppositions and conflicts intertwine within the torrent of destiny, weaving a grand and magnificent tragic epic.

The use of ugliness in performance reflects people's spiritual pursuits and value orientations
"Japanese Butoh" also known as "Dark Butoh" was founded by Kazuo Ohno and Tatsumi Hijikata in 1959. It is regarded as one of the "three major contemporary dance movements". After World War II, the Japanese people were deeply conflicted at heart. They had to bear the consequences of the war, suppress their profound resentment toward the Allied forces, and simultaneously revere Western culture excessively, embracing and flattering it to an extreme degree. Amid this state of profound confusion and contradiction, the Japanese people needed this sense of "rebellion" to reclaim their fragmented sense of national identity as the Yamato people.

From the Butoh performance in the special Tokyo Olympics program Wassai to Mirai Moriyama's solo dance in white during the opening ceremony, these acts sparked ongoing controversy after airing. Some viewers criticized them as "eerie," "creepy," or "incomprehensible." Others defended them, explaining the "rebellious spirit" and "dark aesthetics" of Japanese Butoh, praising it as "sophisticated" and "high art."

However, against the backdrop of the covid, this subjectively perceived ugliness effectively promoted Japanese traditional culture and symbolically conveyed the cycle of life, from birth to passing.

In traditional Chinese drama, the clown character plays an essential role. Clowns bring joy to the audience through humorous and comical performances. With exaggerated and entertaining behavior, clowns excel at creating comedic effects through witty dialogue, body language, and dramatic actions. However, clowns are more than just comedic figures; they often serve as vehicles for social satire. Through their lines and actions, dramas subtly convey dissatisfaction or criticism of social realities. The clown is not only the "comic relief" in Chinese theater but also a symbol of wisdom, critique, and harmony.

The use of ugliness in performance express strong, complex, or unspeakable emotions
Martha Graham's Lamentation portrays sorrow, with the dancer wrapped in taut fabric, each twist and struggle embodying pain and oppression. The movements are sharp and distorted, deviating from traditional grace. Through this raw and unorthodox form of "ugliness," the performance conveys authentic emotion, prioritizing emotional weight over aesthetics and challenging the traditional association of beauty with elegance in dance.

Mary Wigman’s "Witch Dance" and her other works challenged traditional notions of beauty in dance by embracing "ugliness" to reveal the raw, primal essence of human emotions. Critics accused her of abandoning elegance, but Wigman famously responded: "Dance must express human joy, sorrow, and conflict."
In her fight against the vulgarization of dance, Wigman went so far as to forgo musical accompaniment, crafting performances rooted in solemnity, dignity, oppression, darkness, and even ugliness. Her goal was not to please but to awaken audiences to the realities of the era. As American dance critic John Martin eloquently noted: "She leaps from the most delicate, lyrical moods to an obsession with the grotesque and the demonic, only to return to the repression and sublimity of tragedy."

Embracing Diversity

Art is about bold innovation, perhaps nowhere more so than in the world of dance and performance. It is by embracing and understanding various forms of expression—be they deeply rooted in cultural traditions or avant-garde experimentation—that audiences are able to transcend conventional boundaries of perception. This openness enables them to explore the nuances of human experience, gain insights into the rich tapestry of cultures from different backgrounds, and appreciate the profound meanings embedded in performances.

From raw, visceral expressionist works to intricately told stories in classical traditions, diverse dance forms challenge the viewer to reconsider assumptions about beauty, harmony, and emotion. Experimental dance today often employs unconsidered movements, "ugly" aesthetics, or abstract narratives as a means to provoke thought and elicit a deeper emotional response. These forms really push audiences to question not only their aesthetic preferences but also understand the sociocultural and historical contexts that shape such performances.

Such explorations are not only artistic indulgence but hold the potential to transform society. Through diverse expressions, audiences empathize and expand their worldview, forging connections across cultural and ideological divides. In so doing, the diversification of art contributes to the evolution of creative practices and social progress, enabling individuals and communities to negotiate an increasingly complex world.

Art thrives when it dares to break barriers, and dance, with its universal language of movement, stands as a powerful testament to the embracing of diversity for both artistic innovation and societal growth.
